result.markdown:
# Project 2: Logistic Regression Guidelines {#sec-guidelines-logistic}




This chapter walks you through the seven required parts of the logistic regression project. The structure mirrors Project 1, but the statistical methods and reporting conventions differ because the outcome variable is **binary** rather than continuous. Read this chapter carefully before you begin writing.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Review Before You Draft
For the statistical ideas, review [Simple Logistic Regression](https://joonho112.github.io/jlee-regression-course/notes/chapters/09-simple-logistic-regression.html), [Multiple Logistic Regression](https://joonho112.github.io/jlee-regression-course/notes/chapters/10-multiple-logistic-regression.html), and [Logistic Regression Diagnostics](https://joonho112.github.io/jlee-regression-course/notes/chapters/11-logistic-diagnostics.html). For the R workflow, review [Lab 09](https://joonho112.github.io/jlee-regression-course/labs/labs/lab09-logistic-intro.html), [Lab 10](https://joonho112.github.io/jlee-regression-course/labs/labs/lab10-logistic-extended.html), and [Lab 11](https://joonho112.github.io/jlee-regression-course/labs/labs/lab11-logistic-diagnostics.html).
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Overall Page Limits
The main text of Project 2 (Parts 1--6) should be about **3.5 pages** and should not exceed approximately **4 pages** of single-spaced text. Parts 4--5 allow up to **2.5 additional pages** of tables, figures, and essential diagnostic displays. Appendix and reproducibility materials are separate. Quality and clarity matter more than length.
:::


## How This Differs from Project 1 {#sec-logistic-vs-linear}

Before diving into the parts, review the key differences between the two projects:

| Aspect | Project 1 (Linear) | Project 2 (Logistic) |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| Outcome variable | Continuous | Binary (0/1) |
| Coefficients reported | Unstandardized $b$ | **Odds ratios** (OR) |
| Interpretation style | "Y changes by $b$ units" | "Odds of the modeled event multiply by OR" |
| Model fit | $R^2$, adjusted $R^2$, *F*-test | Likelihood-ratio tests, AIC, log-likelihood, pseudo-$R^2$ |
| Key figure | Residual plots | **Predicted probability figure** |
| Diagnostics | Residual normality, constant variance | Sparse cells/separation, collinearity, linearity in the logit, influence |

::: {.callout-tip}
## Combining Both Projects
If you use the **same dataset** for both Project 1 and Project 2, you may combine them into a single report. Parts 1--2 can be shared across both projects, but Parts 3--6 must clearly separate the linear and logistic analyses.
:::


## Part 1: Background and Research Question {#sec-logistic-part1}

*Maximum length: half a page*

Motivate your topic and state a single research question involving a binary outcome. This section is shorter than Project 1 because you only need **one focused research question**.

::: {.callout-note}
## Expectations for Part 1
- Provide a brief paragraph motivating **why** the topic matters.
- Formulate **one research question** that you will address with logistic regression.
- The research question must involve a clearly defined **dichotomous outcome** (e.g., employed vs. not employed, passed vs. failed).
- Name the modeled event explicitly: which outcome category is $Y = 1$?
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Research Question Template
A well-formed research question for a logistic regression project often follows this pattern:

> "What is the relationship between [binary outcome Y] and [predictor X], after controlling for [covariates]?"

or

> "Do the odds of [binary outcome] differ between [groups defined by a categorical predictor], after controlling for [covariates]?"
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## One Research Question, Several Model Stages
Project 2 should state **one focused research question** involving a binary outcome. You may fit a sequence of nested logistic models to build evidence for that question, but the model sequence should support the same overarching question rather than create separate RQ1/RQ2/RQ3 questions. Label the sequence as **Model 1**, **Model 2**, and **Model 3** or as planned model stages, not as separate research questions.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Common Mistakes in Part 1
- **Including more than one research question.** Project 2 requires only one.
- **Choosing an outcome that is not truly binary.** If you are dichotomizing a continuous variable, justify the cutpoint and acknowledge the loss of information.
- **Describing the data here.** Save all data and variable descriptions for Part 2.
:::


## Part 2: Methods --- Subjects and Variables {#sec-logistic-part2}

*Maximum length: half a page*

Describe the data you will analyze. The reader should finish Part 2 knowing exactly what dataset you are using, who the subjects are, and how every variable is measured and coded. Pay special attention to defining the binary outcome.

::: {.callout-note}
## Expectations for Part 2
- Identify the **data source** and briefly describe the study design.
- Describe the **subjects**: who they are, how they were sampled, the starting sample size, any exclusions or restrictions, and the final analytic sample size.
- Report missingness on the outcome and analysis predictors. If you use complete cases, state how many observations remain.
- For the **outcome variable**: clearly define the two categories, specify which is coded 1 (the "event") and which is coded 0, and report the event and non-event counts.
- For **each predictor variable**, provide:
  - The variable name
  - Whether it is **continuous** or **categorical**
  - The measurement scale or unit
  - For categorical predictors: list **all categories** and specify the **reference group**
- If you create dummy variables, dichotomies, centered variables, transformed variables, or merged categories, define each one explicitly and justify the decision.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Organizing Variable Descriptions
A concise table helps the reader quickly grasp your variables:

| Variable | Role | Type | Coding / Description |
|----------|------|------|----------------------|
| `employed_ft` | Outcome | Binary | 1 = employed full-time (event), 0 = not |
| `education` | Predictor | Continuous | Years of formal education |
| `gender` | Predictor | Categorical | Male (ref), Female |
| `age_group` | Predictor | Categorical | 25--34 (ref), 35--44, 45--54 |
:::


## Part 3: Methods --- Statistical Analysis {#sec-logistic-part3}

*Maximum length: half a page*

Tell the reader exactly which logistic regression model or planned model sequence you will fit and connect it to your research question.

::: {.callout-note}
## Expectations for Part 3
- State the logistic regression model you will estimate. You may write it in equation form or in words:
  $$\log\!\left(\frac{p}{1 - p}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \cdots + \beta_k X_k$$
  where $p = P(Y = 1)$.
- **Link the model to your research question.** For example: "To address the research question, we fit a logistic regression predicting full-time employment from education, gender, and age group."
- If you fit nested models, describe the planned sequence and explain what each model stage adds. Use the same complete-case dataset for every nested model in the sequence.
- State the significance level (typically $\alpha = 0.05$).
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Recommended R Workflow
A simple workflow for the logistic project can be built from the same packages used in the labs:

```r
model <- glm(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3, data = analysis_df, family = binomial)

model_params(model, exponentiate = TRUE)
model_fit(model)
performance::check_collinearity(model)

gtsummary::tbl_regression(model, exponentiate = TRUE)
marginaleffects::predictions(model, type = "response")
marginaleffects::plot_predictions(model, condition = "x1", type = "response")
```

For this project guide, use `marginaleffects` as the default package for
predicted probabilities and AMEs. `ggeffects` is also used in the labs for quick
prediction curves, but the goal is not fancy R code; the goal is a clear model,
a clean table, and an interpretable probability-scale result. For an optional
bridge to `marginaleffects::predictions()` syntax, see [Lab 12](https://joonho112.github.io/jlee-regression-course/labs/labs/lab12-logistic-advanced.html);
for final-project templates, use @sec-prediction-templates.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Common Mistakes in Part 3
- **Describing the model without linking it to the research question.** The model must have a clear purpose.
- **Forgetting to mention the significance level.** A simple sentence is sufficient.
:::


## Part 4: Results --- Descriptive Analyses {#sec-logistic-part4}

*Maximum length: half a page of text + up to 1.5 pages of tables and figures*

Descriptive statistics for a logistic regression project focus on how predictor variables relate to the binary outcome.

::: {.callout-note}
## Expectations for Part 4
- Report the **final analytic sample size** and describe any missing data, exclusions, or complete-case restrictions.
- Report the number and percentage of cases in the event and non-event categories.
- For **categorical predictors**: present **cross-tabulations** showing the frequency and percentage of each predictor category within each outcome group (Y = 0 vs. Y = 1).
- For **continuous predictors**: report the mean and standard deviation, separately by outcome group if informative.
- Write a brief narrative summarizing key patterns.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Example Cross-Tabulation

|  | Not Employed FT | Employed FT | Total |
|--|-----------------|-------------|-------|
| Male | 120 (30%) | 280 (70%) | 400 |
| Female | 200 (40%) | 300 (60%) | 500 |
| Total | 320 (36%) | 580 (64%) | 900 |

This type of table immediately shows the reader how the outcome distributes across categories of a predictor.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Merge Sparse Categories
If any cell in a cross-tabulation has **fewer than 5 expected counts**, consider merging that category with a neighboring one. Sparse cells lead to unstable estimates in logistic regression and can cause estimation problems.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Run Descriptives on the Complete-Case Subsample
As with Project 1, your descriptive statistics must be computed on the **same set of cases** that will be used in the regression analysis. If you fit nested models, create the complete-case dataset for the largest planned model and use those same rows for the descriptives and every nested model.
:::


## Part 5: Results --- Regression Analyses {#sec-logistic-part5}

*Maximum length: 1 page of text + up to 1 page of tables and figures*

This is the core of your project. You will fit the logistic regression model or planned model sequence described in Part 3, check model adequacy, and interpret the results using odds ratios and predicted probabilities.

::: {.callout-note}
## Expectations for Part 5
1. **Fit the model or model sequence** from Part 3 and present a publication-style table that includes:
   - **Odds ratios** (OR)
   - 95% confidence intervals for the OR
   - *p*-values
   - model $N$
   - model fit statistics such as AIC, log-likelihood, and pseudo-$R^2$
2. **Check sparse cells and separation**: Report the check and, if problems are present, how you handled zero or near-zero outcome cells, convergence warnings, extremely large coefficients or standard errors, and fitted probabilities near 0 or 1.
3. **Check for collinearity**: Report VIF values. Flag any predictor with VIF > 5.
4. **Check linearity in the logit** for continuous predictors using a planned squared term, binned evidence, a simple diagnostic plot, or another clearly justified check. Avoid adding terms mechanically just to search for significance.
5. **Check influence**: Look for highly influential observations using a diagnostic such as Cook's distance. If any are found, state whether conclusions change in a sensitivity check.
6. **Categorical predictors with 3+ categories**: Report the overall likelihood ratio test (or Wald test) *p*-value for the set of dummy variables, in addition to the individual coefficient tests.
7. **Predicted probabilities**: Include one predicted-probability table for meaningful values or profiles and one predicted-probability figure with 95% confidence intervals.
8. **Interpret focal results** substantively. Report odds ratios in the regression table, but interpret your main finding on both the odds scale and the probability scale using predicted probabilities, probability differences, or average marginal effects.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## How to Describe Odds Ratios
The phrasing depends on the magnitude of the OR:

- **OR between 0.5 and 2.0** --- express as a percentage change in odds:
  - OR = 1.25: "The odds of [outcome] are **25% higher** for [group/unit increase]."
  - OR = 0.80: "The odds of [outcome] are **20% lower** for [group/unit increase]."
- **OR greater than 2.0** --- express as a multiplicative factor:
  - OR = 3.10: "The odds of [outcome] are **3.1 times as large** for [group/unit increase]."
- **OR less than 0.5** --- take the reciprocal for clarity:
  - OR = 0.25 $\Rightarrow$ 1/0.25 = 4.0: "The odds of [outcome] are **4 times as large** for [reference group] compared to [comparison group]."

Always include direction, magnitude, uncertainty, and statistical evidence. Remember that the odds ratio describes the modeled event ($Y = 1$), not the probability itself.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Name the Event Everywhere
All odds ratios, predicted probabilities, marginal effects, ROC/AUC values, sensitivity, and specificity refer to the modeled event, $Y = 1$. State the event explicitly in your table notes, figure captions, and prose. For example, write $P(\text{employed full-time} = 1)$ rather than just "predicted probability."
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Odds Ratio Table Format
A well-organized logistic regression table might look like this:

| Predictor | OR | 95% CI | *p* |
|-----------|------|--------|-------|
| Education (years) | 1.15 | [1.08, 1.23] | < .001 |
| Gender: Female | 0.67 | [0.49, 0.91] | .011 |
| Age: 35--44 | 1.42 | [0.95, 2.12] | .087 |
| Age: 45--54 | 0.88 | [0.58, 1.34] | .548 |

Include model fit statistics (model $N$, AIC, log-likelihood, and pseudo-$R^2$ when available) at the bottom of the table. Report the overall test *p*-value for the age group variable separately.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Predicted Probability Graph
The predicted probability figure is a **required** element of Part 5. Choose a focal predictor or a small set of meaningful profiles that helps answer your research question. The figure should show predicted probabilities of the modeled event ($Y = 1$) with 95% confidence intervals and should clearly state whether other variables are held constant at selected values or averaged over the sample. The curve may look S-shaped, curved, or almost linear over the observed data range; focus on clear interpretation rather than forcing a particular shape.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Prediction Diagnostics Are Optional
The predicted-probability figure is required because it helps readers interpret the model. Classification tables, sensitivity/specificity, cutoff comparisons, ROC/AUC, and calibration plots are optional unless your research question is explicitly about prediction or classification. If you include them, justify the cutoff, discuss base-rate or class-imbalance limits, and remember that AUC measures ranking, not calibration.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Common Mistakes in Part 5
- **Reporting raw log-odds coefficients instead of odds ratios.** Your table must present odds ratios. You may include log-odds in the appendix if you wish, but the main table should use ORs.
- **Forgetting the event definition.** Every OR and predicted probability refers to $Y = 1$.
- **Skipping the collinearity check.** You must report VIF values even if no problems are found.
- **Ignoring sparse cells or separation warnings.** Logistic regression can become unstable when a predictor almost perfectly predicts the outcome.
- **Omitting the predicted probability figure.** This figure is required.
- **Interpreting odds ratios as probabilities.** An OR of 2.0 does *not* mean "twice as likely." It means "twice the *odds*."
- **Forgetting the overall test for multi-category predictors.** If `age_group` has three categories (two dummies), report the overall test *p*-value for the variable as a whole.
:::


## Part 6: Discussion {#sec-logistic-part6}

*Maximum length: half a page*

The discussion ties everything together. Step back from the numbers and address what the results mean in the context of your research question.

::: {.callout-note}
## Expectations for Part 6
- **Answer your research question** by referring to the relevant results from Part 5.
- Discuss the **practical significance** of your findings using predicted probabilities, probability differences, or average marginal effects. Odds ratios are useful, but probability-scale results are usually easier to interpret.
- Note any **limitations** of your analysis (e.g., cross-sectional data, potential confounders, dichotomization choices).
- Be **concise**. Do not repeat the odds ratios from Part 5. Focus on the meaning and implications.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Common Mistakes in Part 6
- **Restating odds ratios verbatim.** The discussion should focus on what the results *mean*, not re-list the numbers.
- **Overclaiming causation.** Unless your study design supports causal inference, use language like "is associated with" rather than "causes."
- **Ignoring non-significant findings.** A non-significant result is still a finding. Briefly discuss what it might mean.
:::


## Part 7: Appendix and Reproducibility Materials {#sec-logistic-part7}

The appendix contains supporting materials that are important but would
interrupt the flow of the main text. Your source file is submitted with the
report as reproducibility evidence.
For diagnostic placement, clean-session checks, and troubleshooting templates,
see @sec-diagnostics-reproducibility-templates.

::: {.callout-note}
## Expectations for Part 7
- **Diagnostic output** that supports Part 5: VIF table, sparse-cell or separation checks, linearity-in-logit evidence, influence diagnostics, and any optional prediction diagnostics you used.
- **Annotated `.qmd` or `.R` source file**: Submit your complete code,
  organized and commented so that a reader could reproduce your analysis. The
  source file should run from start to finish without errors.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Tips for the Source File
- Use **comments** (`#`) generously to explain each step.
- Organize the file in the same order as the project: data loading, data cleaning, complete-case construction, descriptives, logistic regression, diagnostics, predicted probability table, and predicted probability figure.
- Make sure the file runs **from a clean R session** without relying on objects left in your workspace.
- Use relative paths or `here::here()` so the code can be run on another computer.
- Include the code that generates every table and figure used in the report.
:::


## Submission Checklist {#sec-logistic-checklist}

Before you submit Project 2, use the checklist below as the guide's final completeness check. Worked-example checklists show how the examples apply these expectations, but this checklist is the one to use for your own submission. A missing component is the easiest way to lose points.

::: {.callout-important}
## Project 2 Submission Checklist

**Part 1 --- Background and Research Question**

- [ ] Topic is motivated with a clear rationale
- [ ] One focused research question is stated involving a binary outcome
- [ ] Modeled event ($Y = 1$) is named clearly
- [ ] No data descriptions appear in Part 1

**Part 2 --- Methods: Subjects and Variables**

- [ ] Data source and study design are described
- [ ] Starting sample, exclusions, final analytic sample, and missingness are described
- [ ] Outcome variable is defined with both categories, coding (0/1), and event/non-event counts
- [ ] Every predictor lists its name, type, and scale
- [ ] Every categorical predictor has a defined reference category
- [ ] Dummy variables, recodes, transformations, centered variables, or merged categories are explicitly defined

**Part 3 --- Methods: Statistical Analysis**

- [ ] Logistic regression model is stated
- [ ] Model is linked to the research question
- [ ] Nested model stages, if used, support the same research question
- [ ] Same complete-case dataset is used for nested models
- [ ] Significance level ($\alpha$) is stated

**Part 4 --- Results: Descriptive Analyses**

- [ ] Final analytic sample size, missing data, and event/non-event counts are reported
- [ ] Cross-tabulations for categorical predictors vs. binary outcome
- [ ] Means and SDs for continuous predictors (by outcome group if informative)
- [ ] Sparse categories (< 5 expected counts) are merged or addressed
- [ ] Descriptives are based on the complete-case subsample

**Part 5 --- Results: Regression Analyses**

- [ ] Publication-style table with **odds ratios**, 95% CIs, *p*-values, model $N$, and fit statistics
- [ ] Sparse cells, separation, and convergence issues are checked
- [ ] VIF values are reported (collinearity check)
- [ ] Linearity in the logit is checked for continuous predictors
- [ ] Influence diagnostics and sensitivity statement are included
- [ ] Overall test reported for categorical predictors with 3+ categories
- [ ] **Predicted probability table and figure** are included
- [ ] Focal odds ratios are interpreted substantively
- [ ] Main finding is interpreted on the probability scale
- [ ] Optional prediction diagnostics are justified if included

**Part 6 --- Discussion**

- [ ] Research question is addressed
- [ ] Results are discussed in context using probability-scale meaning
- [ ] Limitations are acknowledged

**Part 7 --- Appendix and Reproducibility Materials**

- [ ] Supporting diagnostic output is included
- [ ] Annotated `.qmd` or `.R` source file is included and runs without errors
:::
